title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?

title: Research Areas
text:
I exploit mathematical structures to design and analyze scalable and versatile optimization algorithms, and to learn from high-dimensional data with provable guarantees.
There are a variety of problems at the foundation of Data Science, for which the best problems require ideas and techniques from multiple mathematical disciplines. Currently, I am particularly interested in using ideas from mathematical optimization, geometry, and statistics. Below are some research areas I am actively exploring.

title: Research Areas
text:
Research Areas

Riemannian Optimization
When taught optimization, we are taught many tools and techniques for the following task:
\[
    \min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^d} f(x),
\]
where \(f : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) is some objective function. This lends itself to many different interpretations, depending on your motivations. For example #### Publications

title: Ian McPherson
text:
E-mail
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
      ORCID
  

  
  
Welcome to my website! My name is Ian, and I am currently pursuing a PhD in Applied Mathematics and Statistics under the joint supervision of Mauro Maggioni and Mateo Díaz at Johns Hopkins University. I completed a MSc in Pure Mathematics at Tufts University, and two BAs in Economics and Biochemistry at Occidental College.








I exploit mathematical structures to design and analyze scalable and versatile optimization algorithms, and to learn from high-dimensional data with provable guarantees.



I am interested in the theoretical and algorithmic foundations of Data Science, motivated by the impact of exploiting data for advancing scientific discovery. To exploit data, one must use fast/scalable algorithms while side-stepping the curse of dimensionality which negatively effects convergence and statistical rates.
To this end, I explore the interplay of optimization, geometry, and statistics through a variety of different mathematical lenses including Riemannian geometry, optimal transport, and high-dimensional probability.

Research Areas »




Below are some of my ongoing and recent research projects. For a full list of publications, preprints, and posters, see Publications and Presentations.

Neural Dynamic Portfolio Control with Provable Learning Guarantees | Yizhe Huang, Rui Gao, Shuang Li, Luhao Zhang (Submitted/Under Review) 

Are there neural approaches to portfolio control that incorporate historical returns with provable end-to-end global guarantees? See more… »

Nonsmooth Riemannian Optimization with Inexact Information | Mateo Díaz and Benjamin Grimmer (In-Preparation) 

Can nonsmooth convex Riemannian optimization admit nonasymptotic convergence rates using only subgradients, first-order retractions, and vector transports? See more… »

Wasserstein Barycenters on Unknown Submanifolds of Wasserstein Space | Mauro Maggioni (In-Preparation) 

Given only a local sampler of a non-convex submanifold of Wasserstein space, is there an algorithm to compute the empirical intrinsic mean up to arbitrary accuracy? See more… »

Johns Hopkins University

Bayesian Statistics (553.632)  Fall 2023 | For Professor Sergey Kushnarev | Undergraduate/graduate level course.
Numerical Analysis (553.681)  Spring 2023 | For Professor Mario Micheli | Undergraduate/graduate level course.
Dynamical Systems (553.691)  Fall 2022 | For Professor Yannis Kevrekedis | Undergraduate/graduate level course on nonlinear dynamical systems.

Geometric Toolbox for Data Science (EN.500.111)  Johns Hopkins University | Fall 2024. Designed and taught a survey course in Geometric Methods for High Dimensional Statistics.
Master’s Program Probability Review  Johns Hopkins University | Summer 2023, Summer 2024. Designed and led a review series on probability fundamentals for incoming Master’s students.

text: Optimization in Wasserstein Space

title: Noisy Gradient Descent and Mean Field Langevin Dynamics
text:
ABSTRACT When minimizing \(\mathcal{F} \colon \mathcal{P}_{2}(\mathbb{R}^{d}) \to \mathbb{R}\) over the space of probability measures with finite second moment, a natural ideas is to follows the Wasserstein Gradient Flow. However, this is often computationally infeasible to implement. In this post, we discuss how noisy gradient descent (NGD) can be viewed as a spatial and temporal approximation of Mean Field Langevin Dynamics (MFLD) when applying a entropic regularization to \(\mathcal{F}\).

We begin.
